narrative:
As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many peers would envy. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 65% chance you'll fail the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected—the school uses this tool to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their program.

The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53; this low standing in your later undergraduate performance was the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Your LSAT score played the next biggest role, but in a positive way, pulling the prediction toward success by demonstrating your analytical aptitude compared to typical applicants. Similarly, your above-average undergraduate GPA provided a helpful boost, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass.

In contrast to many students who maintain higher deciles throughout undergrad, your dip in Year 3 stood out as the dominant concern, overshadowing your other strengths despite your middle-income background and full-time commitment, which are common and neutral here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—it's the model weighing patterns from historical data where late-grade struggles correlated strongly with bar outcomes.

To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your later academic deciles, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews that value your LSAT and GPA more flexibly, or gain practical experience like internships to build a stronger narrative. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 performance could open doors—many students in similar spots have turned it around with focused effort. Keep pushing; your baseline strengths position you well for success with the right adjustments.
